1.description: Change in information display. Panel A shows an example of the trading platform screen prior to the salience shock in October 2004. Panel B shows an example of the trading platform screen after the salience shock, when five variables were added: (I) break-even price (column #5), (II) weighted-average purchase price (column #6), (III) paper gain/loss (column #9), (IV) realized gain/loss (column #10), and (V) total gain/loss (column #11). After the salience shock, the font color for each stock was a function of whether the stock was trading at a gain (red) or at a loss (blue). The English translations in both panels and the red rectangles highlighting the added variables in the bottom panel are for display purposes only and were not presented to investors.

2.description: Time series of the average disposition effect. The figure displays the average disposition effect of the internet and noninternet investors in the main branch, estimated from the equationSelli,j,t=α+∑q=2003Q12006Q2βint,qGaini,j,t−1InternetiDq+∑q=2003Q12006Q2βnonint,qGaini,j,t−1NonInternetiDq+Xβ′+εi,j,t,whereSelli,j,tis a dummy variable equal one if investorisells stockjon dayt, Dqis a dummy equal to one if the observation is in quarterq,Internetiis a dummy equal to one ifiis an internet investor,NonInternetiis a dummy equal to one ifiis a noninternet investor, andXis a vector of control variables, including all of the control variables in Ben-David and Hirshleifer (2012), the interaction term betweenInternetiandDq, and the interaction term betweenNonInternetiandDq.βint,qrepresents the average disposition effect for internet investors in quarterqand is plotted in the red time series.βnonint,qrepresents the average disposition effect for noninternet investors in quarterqand is plotted in the blue time series. Colored dotted lines denote 95% confidence intervals around the estimated average disposition effect. The black vertical line denotes the onset of the salience shock.

3.description: Multiple placebo test analysis. This figure presents results of the multiple placebo test analysis using the regression specification in equation1. We run a placebo test on each pair of adjacent quarters in our data set. Specifically, for each pair of adjacent quarters, we define the placebo date as the end of the first quarter and we then conduct the difference-in-differences test. For example, in the first two quarters of the sample, 2003Q1 and 2003Q2, we define the placebo date as the end of 2003Q1. We then conduct the difference-in-differences test and compute thet-statistic on the triple interaction,Gain×Post×Internet. Variable definitions are provided in the caption of TableII. We repeat this procedure for each adjacent set of two quarters and plot the time series oft-statistics. The quarter shown on thex-axis is the second quarter of any of the two adjacent quarters. The bar with horizontal lines denotes the quarter in which the “actual” treatment took place. [Color figure can be viewed atwileyonlinelibrary.com]

4.description: Data on two additional branches—the second branch. The upper panel displays the average disposition effect across all investors from the second branch, estimated from the equationSalei,j,t=α+∑q=2003Q12006Q2βqGaini,j,t−1Dq+Xβ′+εi,j,t,whereSelli,j,tis a dummy variable equal to one if investorisells stockjon dayt. Dqis a dummy equal to one if the observation is in quarterq, andXincludes time fixed effectsDqand the full controls in Ben-David and Hirshleifer (2012).βqrepresents the average disposition effect for all second-branch investors in quarterq, and its time series is plotted in green. The red and blue time series display the disposition effect of internet and noninternet investors from our main branch, as in Figure2. The lower panel provides the time series of internet and noninternet investors after subtracting the disposition effect among investors from the second branch. Colored dotted lines denote 95% confidence intervals around the estimated average disposition effect. The black vertical line denotes the onset of the salience shock.

5.description: Data on two additional branches—the third branch. The upper panel separately displays the average disposition effect of four groups of investors: internet investors from the main branch (solid red line), noninternet investors from the main branch (solid blue line), internet investors from the third branch (dashed red line), and noninternet investors from the third branch (dashed blue line). For each group, the average disposition effect is estimated from the same equation as in Figure2. For each type of investor, internet (solid red line) and noninternet (solid blue line), the lower panel displays the difference in the average disposition effect between the two branches. Colored dotted lines denote 95% confidence intervals around the estimated average disposition effect. The black vertical line denotes the onset of the salience shock.

6.description: Time series of the average disposition effect—the propensity-score-matched sample. The figure displays estimation results from the following equation based on the propensity score–matched sampleSalei,j,t=α+∑q=2003Q12006Q2βint,qGaini,j,t−1InternetiDq+∑q=2003Q12006Q2βnonint,qGaini,j,t−1NonInternetiDq+Xβ′+εi,j,t,whereDqis a dummy equal to one if the observation is in quarter q,Internetiis a dummy equal to one ifiis an internet investor andNonInternetiis a dummy equal to one ifiis a noninternet investor.Xis a vector of control variables, including all of the control variables as Ben-David and Hirshleifer (2012), the interaction term betweenInternetiandDq, and the interaction term betweenNonInternetiandDq.βint,qrepresents the average disposition effect for internet investors in quarterqand is plotted in the red time series.βnonint,qrepresents the average disposition effect for noninternet investors in quarterqand is plotted in the blue time series. Colored dotted lines denote 95% confidence intervals around the estimated average disposition effect. The black vertical line denotes the onset of the salience shock.

7.description: Individual-level estimates of the disposition effect. We estimate the disposition effect for each investor separately for the preperiod and postperiod using equation5. Each point represents an investor. Thex-axis measures the disposition effect in the preperiod while they-axis measures the disposition effect in the postperiod. The black diagonal line is the 45-degree line. [Color figure can be viewed atwileyonlinelibrary.com]
